metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Larch Suillus is treated here as Suillus clintonianus, the North American larch/tamarack-associated species often shown in older guides as Suillus grevillei. Check for larch/tamarack nearby.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when young and carefully prepared, but sticky cap skin and soft texture make it less desirable than prime boletes. Best cooked; peel slimy cap skin if objectionable.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as caution / not for meals in this guide until species-level edibility is clarified.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Larch Suillus.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus clintonianus
use_links.notes: North American larch/tamarack Suillus; separated from European Suillus grevillei.

records.field_identification: Sticky orange-yellow to reddish Suillus associated with larch/tamarack; check for nearby larch and a slimy cap.
records.mushroom_card_note: Fair Suillus tied to larch/tamarack; edible but not a prime bolete.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Edible by many field guides when correctly identified and cooked, but slimy and often modest quality. Many people peel the cap skin and discard old or buggy specimens.
records.notes: In this guide, Slippery Jack means Suillus luteus: the slimy brown-capped, ringed Suillus usually found with pines. Some foragers use “slippery jack” loosely for other slimy-capped Suillus species, so compare the related Suillus entries before assuming a sticky Suillus is this species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Ringed slippery Suillus with a glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, and pine association. Treat the common name broadly only with caution: this page is for Suillus luteus.
mushroom_profile.ring: Ring present; whitish when young, often purple-brown beneath with age
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into fall; often with pine.

records.general_notes: Often abundant under pines. Texture is the limiting factor for many foragers; better young and firm than old and soggy.
records.edibility_detail: Use only when ringed Suillus luteus traits are confirmed: glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, ring, pine association, and no serious decay.
records.species_scope: In this guide, Slippery Jack means Suillus luteus specifically, not every slimy-capped Suillus.
records.look_alike_notes: Related Suillus species and loose “slippery jack” usage: these are comparison links, not a claim that every one is the same species.

related_species.relationship: related Suillus often compared with slippery jacks

related_species.label: White Suillus / Suillus placidus
related_species.relationship: pale sticky Suillus associated with eastern white pine

related_species.label: Larch Suillus / Suillus clintonianus
related_species.relationship: slimy/ringed Suillus associated with larch or tamarack

related_species.label: Butterball / Suillus weaverae
related_species.relationship: sticky Suillus associated with eastern white pine

related_species.label: Chicken-fat Bolete / Suillus americanus
related_species.relationship: yellow/red-fibrillose Suillus associated with white pine

related_species.label: Painted Suillus / Suillus spraguei
related_species.relationship: scaly pink-red Suillus associated with white pine

related_species.label: Dotted-stalk Suillus / Suillus punctipes
related_species.relationship: sticky yellow-brown Suillus with glandular dots

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus luteus
use_links.notes: Ringed Suillus with glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, pine association, and Midwest notes.

use_links.label: First Nature: Suillus luteus / Slippery Jack
use_links.notes: Notes that the common name Slippery Jack can be applied more broadly to several Suillus species.

records.photo_review_cleanup_note: Removed from Needs Review because the only explicit review reasons were image/photo-related and a real Commons-backed image is now available.
records.quality_notes: Condition-sensitive Suillus: young firm specimens can be useful, while older ones become slimy, soggy, buggy, or mushy.
records.eating_use_notes: Use young, firm Suillus luteus; many foragers peel the slimy cap skin. Cook thoroughly and reject old or soggy specimens.
records.risk_reason: Risk is loose common-name use: not every sticky Suillus is Suillus luteus, and poor condition ruins table value.
records.hard_rule: Use only when ringed Suillus luteus traits are confirmed: glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, ring, pine association, and no serious decay.

edible_use.notes: Use young, firm Suillus luteus; many foragers peel the slimy cap skin. Cook thoroughly and reject old, soggy, buggy, or mushy specimens.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — Suillus ID, texture, tolerance, and bolete confusion
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — Suillus ID, texture, tolerance, and bolete confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Suillus risk is usually correct Suillus ID, host association, slimy cap/glandular dots/ring traits, condition, and personal tolerance — not a deadly twin.
required_checks: Host/substrate check required
Pore check required
Confirm Suillus traits such as slime, ring, or glandular dots as appropriate
Reject old, soggy, buggy, mushy specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not collapse all Suillus into “slippery jack”; wrong host, wrong ring/dots, or poor condition means stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

target_traits: Species-specific ring, glandular dots, host tree, and pore size
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not collapse all Suillus into one edible label.

records.source_note: Stale medicinal placeholder text cleared in v4.3.155; image rights metadata left unchanged. v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Edible reports exist for some Suillus, but this one is best treated as a cautious/niche mushroom because texture and ID confidence matter.
records.notes: Blue-staining Slippery Jack: yellow-orange, finely velvety Suillus under two-needle pines, with pore/flesh staining blue.

mushroom_profile.summary: Blue-staining Slippery Jack: yellow-orange, finely velvety Suillus under two-needle pines, with pore/flesh staining blue. Commons-backed images added.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September with associated conifers or pines.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Key marks are the yellow to orangish finely velvety cap, brownish-yellow pores bruising blue, glandular dots on the stem, no ring, and association with jack pine or other two-needle pines.
cap_surface: Yellow to orangish finely velvety cap

records.edibility_detail: Use with caution; a good ID mushroom, not a beginner food shortcut.
records.mushroom_card_note: Blue-staining Slippery Jack: yellow-orange, finely velvety Suillus under two-needle pines, with pore/flesh staining blue. Commons-backed images added.
records.field_identification: Key marks are the yellow to orangish finely velvety cap, brownish-yellow pores bruising blue, glandular dots on the stem, no ring, and association with jack pine or other two-needle pines.

records.notes: Butterball is treated here as Suillus weaverae, the eastern white-pine-associated species that many older North American guides called Suillus granulatus. Host tree is a major clue.

mushroom_profile.summary: Butterball: check Eastern white pine, Pine; Sticky orange-brown cap; Dull yellow pores; and Glandular dots, No ring.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September with eastern white pine.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Use eastern white pine association, sticky orange-brown cap, no ring, small glandular dots on the stem, and dull yellow pore surface.

records.edibility_detail: Occasional/niche use; texture and exact Suillus ID matter.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Chicken Fat Suillus.
staining: May bruise slowly brown to reddish brown
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall with eastern white pine and other 5-needle pines.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.edibility_detail: A white-pine Suillus. Remove slimy skin if desired and cook thoroughly; avoid old soggy specimens.
records.field_identification: Grows with eastern white pine and other 5-needle pines. Bright yellow slimy cap often has red-brown fibers/scales; yellow pores are large and angular; stem has brownish glandular dots and may show veil remnants.

records.edibility_notes: Cooked food. Edible but slimy; usually best peeled and cooked. Remove slimy skin if desired and cook thoroughly; avoid old soggy specimens.

edible_use.notes: Edible but slimy; usually best peeled and cooked. Remove slimy skin if desired and cook thoroughly; avoid old soggy specimens.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible by some when young and well cooked, but quality is modest and individual tolerance varies. Treat it as a cautious edible, avoid old or buggy specimens, and try a small amount the first time.

mushroom_profile.summary: Painted Suillus: colorful, shaggy/scaly red to brick cap with yellow pores and eastern white pine association.
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: high for eastern white pine association
mushroom_profile.ring: Grayish ring or ring zone from partial veil
staining: May bruise reddish or brownish; flesh can stain pinkish to reddish/brownish
Slice and observe staining over time
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall with eastern white pine.
research_notes: MushroomExpert describes eastern white pine association, dry shaggy red/brick cap, yellow pores, ring/ring zone, and reddish-brown spore print.
Photo rights tracked per image for commercial-readiness.
pore_color: Check young and mature pore color
stem_feature: Check scabers / reticulation / glandular dots as appropriate
mushroom_profile.image_coverage_note: Complete: 2 verified Commons/Mushroom Observer images with rights metadata.

records.commonness: Occurs with eastern white pine; locally expected in northern Michigan where suitable host trees occur.
records.edibility_detail: Cautious edible: distinctive but still a Suillus; quality and tolerance vary.
records.field_identification: Look under eastern white pine for a dry shaggy/scaly red to brick-rose cap, pale to yellow pore surface becoming browner with age, angular pores, whitish partial veil remnants when young, a grayish ring or ring zone, and a shaggy reddish-yellow stem without glandular dots. Flesh may stain pinkish, reddish, or brownish when sliced.
records.look_alike_notes: Suillus paluster is smaller and associated with larch/tamarack rather than eastern white pine. Yellow Suillus such as S. americanus differ in cap texture/color pattern and glandular-dot details.
records.overview: Painted Suillus is a colorful eastern-white-pine Suillus with a dry shaggy red to brick-rose cap, yellow pores, a partial veil/ring zone, and a reddish-yellow shaggy stem. It is distinctive among Suillus because it is colorful and not especially slimy.
records.edibility_notes: Reported edible by some when young and well cooked, but quality is modest and individual tolerance varies. Treat it as a cautious edible, avoid old or buggy specimens, and try a small amount the first time.
records.general_notes: Bolete records are kept conservative because host tree, pore color, staining, stem texture, and taste cues can change the ID quickly.

edible_use.notes: Edible by some when young and cooked, but Suillus texture and age matter.

records.mushroom_card_note: Colorful white-pine Suillus with shaggy reddish cap scales, yellow pores, and a ringed or veiled stem.

records.culinary_uses: Peel the slimy cap cuticle if desired, remove the tube layer from older specimens, and cook thoroughly. Best used young and firm.
records.notes: Short-stalked Suillus with a very slimy brown cap, yellow pores, no ring, and hard-pine association. Do not rely on shortness alone; host pine and cap/stem details matter.

pore_color: Pale yellow to dingy yellow / brownish yellow with age
staining: Flesh generally not staining when sliced
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall under hard pines.

records.food_quality: Good to choice for some, but Suillus texture caveats apply

records.mushroom_card_note: Short-stalked pine Suillus with a slimy brown cap, yellow pores, very subtle glandular dots, and hard-pine association.
records.field_identification: Look under two- or three-needle pines for a very slimy dark brown to reddish-brown cap, pale yellow pores that do not stain strongly, no ring, and glandular dots on the stem that may be nearly invisible without a hand lens. Do not use stem shortness by itself as the ID.
records.edibility_detail: Edible for some after peeling/slime removal and cooking, but not a prime target for everyone.

records.overview: A slimy brown hard-pine Suillus with yellow pores, no ring, and often a short, squat stem. It is edible in many guides, but the slimy cap skin and older tube layer are commonly removed.
records.edibility_notes: Caution-level edible. Some people dislike or react to Suillus texture/slime. Avoid old, soggy, bug-ridden, or uncertain specimens.
records.look_alike_notes: Compare with Suillus granulatus/weaverae, veiled Suillus species, and other pine-associated slippery jacks. Host pine type, ring/veil presence, glandular dots, pore surface, and cap slime matter.
records.photo_completion_note: 2 distinct verified image(s) with commercial-use rights metadata.
records.completion_scope: current app schema: practical ID, edibility/caution, look-alikes, source links, verified structured photos, and commercial-use photo-rights metadata

mushroom_profile.summary: Granulated Suillus: sticky orangish-brown cap, yellow non-bruising pores with milky droplets when young, no ring, and glandular dots.
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: medium_for_true_species; caution for white-pine North American records
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October; summer into fall with pines where locally established.
research_notes: MushroomExpert notes true S. granulatus is associated with 2-needled pines and that white-pine-associated North American field-guide material is often S. weaverae.
Photo rights tracked per image for commercial-readiness.
pore_color: Yellow pores; milky droplets when young
mushroom_profile.image_coverage_note: Complete: 3 verified Commons images with rights metadata.

records.general_notes: 3.60. Use host, ring absence, yellow pores, and glandular/stem granules together; do not rely on cap color alone.
records.commonness: Uncertain locally; true species may be uncommon or introduced in North America.
records.mushroom_card_note: Granulated Suillus: sticky orangish-brown cap, yellow non-bruising pores with milky droplets when young, no ring, and glandular dots.
records.field_identification: Confirm pine association first. For true Suillus granulatus, expect a sticky orangish-brown cap, small yellow non-bruising pores that may exude milky droplets when young, no stem ring, and glandular dots on the stem that darken with age. Treat white-pine-associated North American collections cautiously because similar material may be Suillus weaverae.
records.edibility_detail: Cautious edible: identification and host tree matter, quality is modest, and individual tolerance varies.

records.overview: Granulated Suillus / weeping bolete is a pine-associated Suillus with a sticky orangish-brown cap, small yellow pores that may bead milky droplets when young, no ring, and glandular dots on the stem. In North America, records need host caution because white-pine collections often belong near Suillus weaverae rather than true European Suillus granulatus.
records.edibility_notes: Edible in some guides when young and thoroughly cooked, but not a high-value target here. Peel very slimy cap skin if desired, avoid old or waterlogged specimens, and try only a small amount the first time because Suillus species can bother some people.
records.look_alike_notes: White-pine-associated North American material often belongs near Suillus weaverae. Compare ringless/ringed Suillus species carefully, and do not use cap color alone.

use_links.notes: Sticky cap, yellow non-bruising pores, milky droplets, no ring, glandular dots, and North American host caution.
